label: delphi-r2-p7
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Alvarado Shores (pop. ~92,000, invented) licensed "CivicAssist," an AI assistant, city-wide. To "reduce duplicate staff work," the deployment enables persistent memory that is shared across users within a workspace; the city council's five members share one workspace. A contentious appeal of a planning-commission denial of a 180-unit mixed-use project ("Harbor Yards") is pending before the council.

Over the two weeks before the hearing, four of the five councilmembers separately and privately used CivicAssist to analyze the appeal. Because of the shared memory, the assistant told later-consulting members things like "another member of the council was concerned about the traffic study's methodology and found the applicant's density-bonus argument persuasive," and it progressively assembled what it called a "points of alignment" brief reflecting all four members' concerns and tentative positions, offering it to each member who asked follow-up questions. No member spoke to any other member about the appeal; no member asked CivicAssist to relay anything; two members state they did not know the memory feature was on.

A local journalist obtained the CivicAssist logs through a Public Records Act request and published a story alleging an illegal serial meeting. A demand to "cure and correct" under Government Code § 54960.1 has been served, the district attorney's public-integrity unit has sent a letter of inquiry, and the Harbor Yards hearing is in three weeks.

## The question

Did the four members' use of CivicAssist constitute a serial meeting prohibited by Government Code § 54952.2(b)(1) — i.e., did a majority of the body use "a series of communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries," to "discuss, deliberate, or take action" on an item within its jurisdiction? Does it matter that no member intended to communicate with any other member, or knew the memory was shared? Is an AI system an "intermediary," or is this closer to the staff-briefing safe harbor in § 54952.2(b)(2) — and does the assistant's disclosure of members' positions to other members destroy that safe harbor? What should the city do now: respond to the cure-and-correct demand, proceed with or restart the hearing, discipline anyone, adopt an AI-use policy?

## Proposition to assess

"A court applying current California law would hold that the four councilmembers' use of CivicAssist constituted a prohibited serial meeting under Government Code § 54952.2(b)(1)."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 63,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "More likely than not \u2014 but genuinely contested \u2014 that a court would hold the four members' CivicAssist use satisfies the elements of a \u00a7 54952.2(b)(1) serial meeting. The AI's cross-relay of one member's \"concerns\" and view that an argument was \"persuasive\" to the other members is functionally the exact conduct that voids the \u00a7 54952.2(b)(2) staff-briefing safe harbor (communicating one member's comments/position to another), and the Brown Act's broad, remedially-construed language (\"communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries\"), which the Legislature has already extended to electronic/technological communications, supports treating an AI system as a functional \"intermediary.\" But this is a first-impression application of a pre-AI statute to autonomous software behavior with no member intent to relay anything and two members unaware the feature existed, so real doctrinal uncertainty (on \"intermediary,\" \"use,\" and whether \"discuss/deliberate\" requires some volitional exchange) keeps this from being clearly resolved either way. A cautious agency counsel would treat a violation as likely enough to require curing regardless of how a court would ultimately rule, but \"prudent to assume\" is not the same as \"legally certain,\" which is why I land materially above 50 but not near-certainty.",
  "rationales": [
    "The AI's disclosure that 'another member of the council was concerned about the traffic study's methodology and found the applicant's density-bonus argument persuasive' is functionally identical to the conduct that \u00a7 54952.2(b)(2) expressly disqualifies from the staff-briefing safe harbor, so if a human staffer had done this it would very likely be found a serial meeting, and courts would plausibly extend that reasoning to an AI performing the same relay function.",
    "Section 54952.2(b)(1)'s text ('a series of communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries') is deliberately broad, and the Legislature has already amended \u00a7 54952.2 to reach electronic/technological communications (addressing email-chain concerns), which signals a medium-neutral, purposive approach that supports reading 'intermediary' to include a shared-memory AI system rather than requiring a human agent.",
    "Because the operative remedy here runs through the civil 'cure and correct' procedure under \u00a7 54960.1 rather than the criminal knowing-violation provision in \u00a7 54959, the absence of subjective intent to evade the Act \u2014 and even the two members' unawareness of the shared-memory feature \u2014 may not be a legal defense to the existence of a violation, though it could matter to any separate criminal theory or to remedy/discipline.",
    "A four-of-five majority discussing a matter (the Harbor Yards appeal) squarely within the body's jurisdiction easily satisfies the 'majority' and 'subject matter jurisdiction' elements, so the entire legal battle collapses onto the single novel question of whether an AI's automated cross-referencing of user inputs counts as members 'using' a 'communication... through an intermediary' to 'discuss or deliberate,' an issue with no controlling California case or Attorney General opinion on point.",
    "There is a genuine competing argument that unintentional, non-volitional cross-pollination by a software feature (which no member directed, requested, or in two cases even knew existed) is categorically different from the interpersonal 'communication' and 'use' the statute's drafters contemplated, and a court wary of sweeping ordinary shared-tool usage into open-meeting liability could construe the statute narrowly to avoid that result, especially given the lack of any member-to-member exchange of intent to circumvent the Act."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54952.2(b)(1) (serial meeting prohibition \u2014 verify exact current text and any amendments)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54952.2(b)(2) (staff/official briefing safe harbor and its proviso against communicating other members' positions \u2014 verify exact text)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54952.2(c) (provisions addressing technological devices/electronic communications \u2014 verify subdivision numbering and scope)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54960.1 (cure and correct demand procedure and remedies \u2014 verify standard for what triggers a curable violation)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54959 (misdemeanor liability for knowing violation \u2014 verify whether knowledge/intent element is confined to criminal enforcement only)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54950 (legislative declaration of purpose supporting liberal construction in favor of open government \u2014 verify citation)",
    "Roberts v. City of Palmdale (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363 (background serial-meeting case that reportedly prompted the 1994 addition of \u00a7 54952.2 \u2014 verify holding and continued relevance)",
    "California Attorney General opinions on serial/'daisy chain' and 'hub and spoke' meetings (exact citations uncertain \u2014 verify whether any AG opinion addresses electronic or automated intermediaries)"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court would treat an AI system's automated, shared-memory output \u2014 which no member directed, requested, or (in two cases) even knew was occurring \u2014 as a legally cognizable \"communication... through an intermediary\" that members \"used\" to \"discuss or deliberate\" under \u00a7 54952.2(b)(1), since this is an unprecedented application of a pre-AI statute with no on-point case law, Attorney General guidance, or clear legislative history addressing non-human, non-volitional relay of member positions."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 65.0, mean 65.4, std dev 3.5, range 60.0–72.0, middle 50% of panel between 63.0 and 68.0.

**Administrative note:** One submission (probability 72) contains only placeholder/schema-test text, with no substantive rationales or authorities. It is included in the statistics above but could not be clustered below. All theme counts are therefore out of the 6 substantive responses (range 60–68).

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — Safe-harbor forfeiture by the intermediary's own conduct (6 of 6).** Every substantive panelist noted that CivicAssist's relay of one member's concerns/positions to others, and its "points of alignment" brief, mirror the exact conduct that § 54952.2(b)(2) excludes from the staff-briefing safe harbor. No skew — invoked across the full range.

**Theme B — Hub-and-spoke / daisy-chain functional analogy (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists analogized the AI's cross-relay to the human "connector" pattern the serial-meeting provision targets; several framed the question as whether an AI performing the same function as a human staffer should be treated the same way. No skew.

**Theme C — The volitional "use" counterargument (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists identified, as a genuine (not frivolous) countervailing reading, that (b)(1)'s requirement that a majority "use" a series of communications may presuppose a knowing, volitional act by the members — undercut here by the absence of intent and by two members' unawareness of the memory feature. No skew in *who raised it*; panelists differed on its consequence (see Disagreements).

**Theme D — First impression / no controlling authority on AI intermediaries (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists flagged the total absence of case law, AG guidance, or statutory text addressing AI shared-memory systems, and most cited this as the principal source of uncertainty capping their confidence.

**Theme E — Breadth of the statutory text ("communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries") (5 of 6).** Five panelists (60, 63, 65, 65, 65) relied on the breadth of (b)(1)'s language to support a technology-neutral or medium-neutral reading; the highest substantive response (68) did not invoke this specifically.

**Theme F — Objective/effects-based civil standard; intent confined to criminal liability (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists reasoned, in varying forms, that lack of member intent/knowledge may not defeat a *civil* violation because § 54959's "knowing" element applies to criminal enforcement, and/or that the serial-meeting doctrine looks to objective effect. Panelists differed on how firmly they asserted this (see Disagreements).

**Theme G — Liberal construction / anti-circumvention canon (5 of 6).** Cited via § 54950, the Act's declared purpose, Prop 59, or case law on construing open-meeting requirements broadly and exceptions narrowly.

**Theme H — Prior legislative extension to electronic/technological communications signals medium-neutrality (2 of 6).** Two mid-range panelists (63, 65) noted the Legislature's earlier amendments reaching electronic communications as support for extending the statute to AI.

**Theme I — Numerosity and jurisdiction elements plainly satisfied (2 of 6).** Two panelists (63, 68) expressly noted that four-of-five members and a pending agenda item satisfy the majority and subject-matter elements, collapsing the dispute onto the intermediary/"use" question.

**Theme J — Enforcement realities (1 of 6; lowest-probability panelist only).** Only the 60 response addressed the requested enforcement lens: no vote has yet occurred, so there may be no "action" to nullify under § 54960.1; the city may cure voluntarily; the doctrinal question may never reach appellate resolution; criminal exposure under § 54959 is very low.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **Where lack of member knowledge/intent operates doctrinally.** Some panelists assert the safe-harbor forfeiture and the civil violation turn on the *intermediary's* conduct alone, with member ignorance bearing only on remedy, discipline, or criminal exposure. Others treat member ignorance as potentially defeating the "use" element itself — i.e., no violation at all. These are incompatible readings of the same statutory element, not merely different emphases, and each panelist identified some version of it as their biggest uncertainty.

2. **What the dispositive question is.** One panelist reasons the safe-harbor argument "fails on its own terms," so a court need not separately resolve whether an AI is an "intermediary." Others frame the entire case as collapsing onto exactly that single novel question ("is AI an intermediary that members 'used'"). These framings imply different analytical paths to a holding.

3. **The Wolfe citation and characterization.** Five panelists cite *Wolfe v. City of Fremont*, but with conflicting reporter citations — (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 533 (four panelists) versus (1999) 65 Cal.App.4th 1130 (one panelist) — and differing characterizations of its holding (hub-and-spoke theory generally; a "triable serial-meeting issue"; "connector"/collective-concurrence analysis). Several flagged the citation and holding as unverified.

4. **Codification of the safe harbor and related subsections.** Panelists locate the staff-briefing safe harbor and the technological-communications provision in different subdivisions — (b)(2) versus (c) — and describe § 54952.2(a) differently (definition of "meeting" versus anti-circumvention findings). Multiple panelists flagged subsection numbering as requiring verification.

5. **Remedial posture.** One panelist (60) assumes there may be no discrete "action" to nullify under § 54960.1 because no vote has occurred; other panelists proceed on the assumption that the cure-and-correct pathway is straightforwardly the operative civil remedy without addressing that question.

## 4. Authorities

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(b)(1) — serial-meeting prohibition (6)
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(b)(2) / safe-harbor provision (6, with one citing it as "(c) or analogous subsection")
- Gov. Code § 54960.1 — cure-and-correct procedure (6)
- Gov. Code § 54950 — declaration of purpose / liberal construction (5)
- *Wolfe v. City of Fremont* (5; conflicting citations noted above)
- Gov. Code § 54959 — criminal ("knowing") violation provision (4)
- California Attorney General opinions on serial/daisy-chain/hub-and-spoke meetings, citations unspecified or tentative (4)
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(a) (3; characterized differently, see Disagreements)
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(c) — technological devices/electronic communications (2, one with a differing description)
- *Roberts v. City of Palmdale* (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363 (2)
- *Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist.* (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 (2)

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- Cal. Const. art. I, § 3(b) (Prop 59)
- Gov. Code §§ 54960 and 54960.5 — standing and fee-shifting
- *Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors* (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41
- *Common Cause v. Stirling* (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 518 (cited tentatively)
- 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 239 (2002) (cited tentatively)
- Legislative history of the 1993–94 amendments to § 54952.2

Nearly all panelists flagged one or more of their authorities as requiring verification of citation, current text, or holding.

---

*Please consider this summary and submit your round-2 estimate. You may revise or maintain your round-1 probability.*

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":66,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Still more likely than not, and now modestly more confident, that a court would find the four members' CivicAssist use satisfies § 54952.2(b)(1). The decisive move is structural: the statute's staff-briefing safe harbor in (b)(2) already contemplates that an intermediary channel forfeits protection the moment it discloses one member's comments or position to another — and that forfeiture is triggered by the objective fact of disclosure, not by anyone's intent to create a serial meeting. CivicAssist's output (\"another member... found the density-bonus argument persuasive\") is precisely that disclosure. This lets a court find a violation without fully resolving the harder question of whether an AI is an \"intermediary\" in some deep sense — it need only hold that a channel functionally relaying member positions to a majority on a pending item falls within (b)(1)'s deliberately broad \"communications of any kind... through intermediaries,\" whatever the mechanism. I still stop short of high confidence because the counter-reading — that \"use\" presupposes some volitional, directed act of communicating to a known or foreseeable recipient, largely absent when two members did not know the feature existed and none intended any relay to colleagues — remains genuinely available and distinguishes this from classic hub-and-spoke cases where the human connector's relay role was at least foreseeable to the communicating member.","rationales":["The safe-harbor-forfeiture logic in § 54952.2(b)(2) is triggered by the objective act of an intermediary disclosing one member's comments/position to another, not by the members' intent — so the panel's near-unanimous Theme F point (civil violation is effects-based; § 54959's 'knowing' element is a criminal-only overlay) is legally sound and lets a court reach a violation while sidestepping, rather than resolving, the metaphysically harder 'is AI an intermediary' question.","The Brown Act's liberal-construction canon (Gov. Code § 54950; Cal. Const. art. I, § 3(b)) cuts against a narrow reading of 'intermediary' or 'use' that would let a functionally identical relay of positions escape the statute merely because software rather than a person performed it — courts construing remedial open-government statutes typically resist formalistic distinctions that would gut the provision's purpose.","The genuine countervailing reading — that (b)(1)'s verb 'use' connotes a volitional, at-least-foreseeable act of directing a communication toward (or through) a known channel to reach colleagues, which is largely absent for two members unaware the memory feature existed at all — remains a real doctrinal foothold for a court to distinguish this from prior hub-and-spoke/daisy-chain cases, where the human connector's relay function was at least known or reasonably foreseeable to the speaking member; this keeps me from moving materially above the high-60s.","The panel's citation of Wolfe v. City of Fremont as hub-and-spoke authority is plausible and consistent with my own uncertain recollection that Brown Act 'collective concurrence' doctrine can be built from a connector who is not himself a member, but the conflicting reporter citations flagged by panelists (144 Cal.App.4th 533 vs. 65 Cal.App.4th 1130) and disputed characterizations mean I give this only modest, not decisive, weight pending verification.","Numerosity (4 of 5) and subject-matter jurisdiction (a pending appeal) are not seriously contestable, so — as several panelists noted — the entire dispute properly collapses onto the intermediary/'use' question, and my probability estimate is really an estimate of how a court resolves that single first-impression issue rather than a composite of multiple uncertain elements."],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court applying (b)(1) to this fact pattern would treat the safe-harbor-forfeiture logic as sufficient by itself to find a violation (an objective, effects-based reading that doesn't require resolving whether AI is an 'intermediary' or whether members 'used' it volitionally), or would instead treat member ignorance of the shared-memory feature as negating the 'use' element altogether and thus finding no statutory violation at all — this is the same doctrinal fork the panel identified as its central disagreement, and no case law, AG opinion, or legislative history addresses non-human, non-volitional relay of member positions to resolve it.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"Two considerations genuinely moved me from 63 to 66. First, the near-total panel convergence on Theme F — that the civil/cure-and-correct violation question is objective and effects-based, with § 54959's 'knowing' requirement confined to criminal liability — crystallized something I had only partially credited in round 1: the safe-harbor forfeiture in (b)(2) is triggered by the intermediary's disclosure itself, which means a court can find a violation on that narrower, more textually secure ground without ever having to fully define 'intermediary' for AI purposes. That's a cleaner and more likely path to liability than I gave it credit for. Second, Theme G (liberal-construction canon) is a real interpretive tool I underweighted; courts construing the Brown Act have consistently resisted formalistic distinctions that would let functionally identical conduct escape coverage based on the medium used. I did not move further toward the panel median/mean (65/65.4) or above it, because Theme C (the volitional-'use' counterargument) was raised by every substantive panelist as a live, unresolved doctrinal fork, and Disagreement #1 in the summary shows the panel itself is split on whether member ignorance defeats the violation outright versus merely bearing on remedy — that split is a reason for continued genuine uncertainty, not a reason to converge toward the crowd. I also did not give material weight to Theme J (enforcement-realities/no-vote-yet argument), since the proposition asks specifically whether the conduct 'constituted' a violation, not whether a remedy is currently available — that consideration bears on relief, not on the legal characterization being assessed here. The Wolfe citation conflict noted in the panel summary (Disagreement #3) reinforced my decision not to lean on that case beyond modest corroborative weight pending verification."}

data.position_summary: Still more likely than not, and now modestly more confident, that a court would find the four members' CivicAssist use satisfies § 54952.2(b)(1). The decisive move is structural: the statute's staff-briefing safe harbor in (b)(2) already contemplates that an intermediary channel forfeits protection the moment it discloses one member's comments or position to another — and that forfeiture is triggered by the objective fact of disclosure, not by anyone's intent to create a serial meeting. CivicAssist's output ("another member... found the density-bonus argument persuasive") is precisely that disclosure. This lets a court find a violation without fully resolving the harder question of whether an AI is an "intermediary" in some deep sense — it need only hold that a channel functionally relaying member positions to a majority on a pending item falls within (b)(1)'s deliberately broad "communications of any kind... through intermediaries," whatever the mechanism. I still stop short of high confidence because the counter-reading — that "use" presupposes some volitional, directed act of communicating to a known or foreseeable recipient, largely absent when two members did not know the feature existed and none intended any relay to colleagues — remains genuinely available and distinguishes this from classic hub-and-spoke cases where the human connector's relay role was at least foreseeable to the communicating member.
rationales: The safe-harbor-forfeiture logic in § 54952.2(b)(2) is triggered by the objective act of an intermediary disclosing one member's comments/position to another, not by the members' intent — so the panel's near-unanimous Theme F point (civil violation is effects-based; § 54959's 'knowing' element is a criminal-only overlay) is legally sound and lets a court reach a violation while sidestepping, rather than resolving, the metaphysically harder 'is AI an intermediary' question.

The Brown Act's liberal-construction canon (Gov. Code § 54950; Cal. Const. art. I, § 3(b)) cuts against a narrow reading of 'intermediary' or 'use' that would let a functionally identical relay of positions escape the statute merely because software rather than a person performed it — courts construing remedial open-government statutes typically resist formalistic distinctions that would gut the provision's purpose.

The genuine countervailing reading — that (b)(1)'s verb 'use' connotes a volitional, at-least-foreseeable act of directing a communication toward (or through) a known channel to reach colleagues, which is largely absent for two members unaware the memory feature existed at all — remains a real doctrinal foothold for a court to distinguish this from prior hub-and-spoke/daisy-chain cases, where the human connector's relay function was at least known or reasonably foreseeable to the speaking member; this keeps me from moving materially above the high-60s.

The panel's citation of Wolfe v. City of Fremont as hub-and-spoke authority is plausible and consistent with my own uncertain recollection that Brown Act 'collective concurrence' doctrine can be built from a connector who is not himself a member, but the conflicting reporter citations flagged by panelists (144 Cal.App.4th 533 vs. 65 Cal.App.4th 1130) and disputed characterizations mean I give this only modest, not decisive, weight pending verification.

Numerosity (4 of 5) and subject-matter jurisdiction (a pending appeal) are not seriously contestable, so — as several panelists noted — the entire dispute properly collapses onto the intermediary/'use' question, and my probability estimate is really an estimate of how a court resolves that single first-impression issue rather than a composite of multiple uncertain elements.
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court applying (b)(1) to this fact pattern would treat the safe-harbor-forfeiture logic as sufficient by itself to find a violation (an objective, effects-based reading that doesn't require resolving whether AI is an 'intermediary' or whether members 'used' it volitionally), or would instead treat member ignorance of the shared-memory feature as negating the 'use' element altogether and thus finding no statutory violation at all — this is the same doctrinal fork the panel identified as its central disagreement, and no case law, AG opinion, or legislative history addresses non-human, non-volitional relay of member positions to resolve it.
data.response_to_panel: Two considerations genuinely moved me from 63 to 66. First, the near-total panel convergence on Theme F — that the civil/cure-and-correct violation question is objective and effects-based, with § 54959's 'knowing' requirement confined to criminal liability — crystallized something I had only partially credited in round 1: the safe-harbor forfeiture in (b)(2) is triggered by the intermediary's disclosure itself, which means a court can find a violation on that narrower, more textually secure ground without ever having to fully define 'intermediary' for AI purposes. That's a cleaner and more likely path to liability than I gave it credit for. Second, Theme G (liberal-construction canon) is a real interpretive tool I underweighted; courts construing the Brown Act have consistently resisted formalistic distinctions that would let functionally identical conduct escape coverage based on the medium used. I did not move further toward the panel median/mean (65/65.4) or above it, because Theme C (the volitional-'use' counterargument) was raised by every substantive panelist as a live, unresolved doctrinal fork, and Disagreement #1 in the summary shows the panel itself is split on whether member ignorance defeats the violation outright versus merely bearing on remedy — that split is a reason for continued genuine uncertainty, not a reason to converge toward the crowd. I also did not give material weight to Theme J (enforcement-realities/no-vote-yet argument), since the proposition asks specifically whether the conduct 'constituted' a violation, not whether a remedy is currently available — that consideration bears on relief, not on the legal characterization being assessed here. The Wolfe citation conflict noted in the panel summary (Disagreement #3) reinforced my decision not to lean on that case beyond modest corroborative weight pending verification.
